title: Hippocampal Slow Waves
abstract:
Hippocampal Slow Waves
Distribution and Phase Relationships in the Course of Approach Learning
W. R. ADEY, M.D.; C. W. DUNLOP, Ph.D., and C. E. HENDRIX, M.S., Los Angeles
The functions of the temporal lobe, and
its preponderant role in the integrative as-
pects of social behavior,
are well known
(Bucy and Kl\l=u"\ver,13 1939) and have been
the subject of recent reviews (Adey,2 1959;
Baldwin and Bailey,8 1958).
To some ex-
tent, physiological dissection of the picture
of "psychic
blindness" characterizing the
complete bilateral temporal lobectomy has
been possible with more limited resections,
with aspects of hypersexuality, loss of mem-
ory,
taming,
and
exaggerated
rage
re-
sponses,
each
reported
as
the
dominant
aspect of
a variety of limited resections in
man and animals (Adey,1 1958; Bard and
Mountcastle,10 1948; Green, Clemente, and
de Groot,24 1958;
Schreiner and Kling,45
1953;
Scoville and Milner,46 1957).
Historically, the limbic lobe, set as a ring
of cortex at the medial margin of the hemi-
sphere,
has
been
described
as
a somatic
integrating center concerned with the inte-
gration of somatic influxes with olfactory
and a variety of nominally "visceral" inputs
(Broca,12 1878; Elliot Smith,17 1910; Her¬
rick,26 1933;
Maclean,37 1955).
To
some
extent, attention of physiologists has been
focused
on aspects of
sensory inputs
to
these
areas, although Elliot Smith, many
years ago, stressed the significance of the
fornix
as
an
efferent pathway
from the
primitive hippocampal cortex of the sub-
mammalian vertebrate, and emphasized its
originally uninterrupted course to
a termi¬
nation in midbrain tegmental centers, which,
in turn, project to spinal levels.
It
is
to the possible
functions of
the
hippocampus in integrated motor behavior,
and to the correlates of its electrical activity
with the execution of planned motor acts,
that the present study has been mainly di¬
rected.
The characteristic slow-wave trains
reported in the hippocampus during noxious
stimulation (Jung and Kornmüller,30 1939)
and alerting and an arousal (Green and Ar-
duini,23 1954)
disappear following septal
lesions (Green and Arduini23), but persist
after
ablation
of
the posterior pyriform
(entorhinal) cortex (Adey, Sunderland, and
Dunlop,7 1957).
Anatomical investigations have suggested
two clearly different, and, indeed, oppositely
directed
pathways
for
fluxes
passing
through the hippocampus (Ramón y Cajal,14
1909; Elliot Smith,17 1910; Adey,2 1959).
Electrode
placements
and
computing
of
phase relationships in this study have been
designed to test these hypotheses.
It has
also been widely assumed that a functional
homogeneity characterized the hippocampus
as
a whole, presumably by
reason of its
apparent structural homogeneity.
Results
presented here in recordings from the dor¬
sal and ventral parts of the hippocampal
arch do not support such a view. It appears,
rather, that there is
a considerable func¬
tional differentiation, both within the cell
groupings at any one level and from differ¬
ent parts of the hippocampal arch
as
a
whole.
Methods
Eighteen
cats
have
been used
in
the present
study.
In
all bipolar recording
electrodes
have
Accepted for publication Jan. 18, 1960.
This work was assisted by Grants B-610, B-611,
and B-1883 from the National Institutes of Health
of the U.S. Public Health Service, and by a grant
from the Ford Foundation.
Departments
of
Anatomy,
Engineering,
and
Physiology, University of California at Los An-
geles,
and Veterans Administration Hospitals
at
Sawtelle and Long Beach, Calif.
Downloaded From: http://archneur.jamanetwork.com/ by a CAMBRIDGE UNIVERSITY LIBRARY User  on 05/29/2015
 been implanted stereotaxically.
Each bipole
was
formed of two 36-gauge stainless-steel wires, sup¬
ported by and projecting beyond a Nichrome staff
for
a
distance of 2-3
mm.
Tips of
the wires
were separated by 1.5 mm.
A maximum of eight
bipolar electrodes were inserted in any one brain.
Approximately
one month elapsed postoperatively
before
commencement of recording,
and
no de¬
terioration in quality of records was observed in
cats kept as long as nine months.
Since
interest
centered primarily
in
the hip¬
pocampal slow-wave trains of between 2 and 5
waves/sec, it
was necessary to
use
a relatively
long
time
constant
(0.3
sec.)
for satisfactory
reproduction of these wave trains.
This, in turn,
imposed stringent requirements
on the elimination
of movement artifacts and artifacts arising from
static changes
on the animal's fur.
After
con¬
siderable trial,
a satisfactory solution was found,
with two main features.
The connecting cable to
the animal
was prepared from
a special double-
shielded wire, having
a conducting layer between
inner and outer sheaths (Microdot Corporation)
which effectively dissipated
static
changes
pro¬
duced by deformation of the cable. A metal tray,
carefully insulated from all surrounding objects,
formed the bottom of the test box and effectively
eliminated movement artifacts.
To assess accurately the phases in the animal's
performance in the test box, photographs at 2 or
4 frames/sec. were taken of activity reflected from
a mirror placed above the test box, using a Grass
kymograph
camera.
A marker pulse
from
the
camera provided
accurate indexing of
the film
with the EEG record.
Eight-channel Grass and
six-channel Offner electroencephalographs recorded
the cerebral activity.
Histologie examination by serial frozen sections
was performed at the conclusion of all training
experiments
and
the
placement
of
electrodes
checked.
Training Procedures.—These involved
the
use
of a T-box, 1.5 meters long, in which a partition
divided
the
box
at
one
end
into
right
and
left halves.
An illuminated rectangle placed above
a concealed food reward provided
a visual
cue.
The reward (and visual cue)
was changed from
side to side randomly.
In testing, the animal was
placed behind
an opaque door at the other end
of the box.
Two basic procedures were used in
sequencing
the presentation of
stimuli.
In
one,
a 500-cycle tone was switched off at the moment
the cue light was turned on and the doors opened
to present the situation to the animal.
The visual
cue remained
on and the
tone off for
a period
of five seconds, during which the animal was
re¬
quired
to seek the reward.
With most animals,
an incorrect choice
on the first approach meant
the loss of the reward for that run. In the other
procedure the light and tone were paired and pre¬
sented concurrently throughout the test run.
The
duration
of
the
test
epoch
and
all switching
associated with the presentation of light and tone
were arranged through Grass Model IV C stimu¬
lators and appropriate relays.
The test box was
operated inside a shielded room.
Electrode Placements.—Before we proceed to a
discussion of our findings, and in order to elucidate
the rationale of electrode placements,
a brief de¬
scription will be given of the functional anatomy
probably underlying the interrelations between the
hippocampus and the adjacent pyriform (entorhinal)
cortex. Aspects of intrahippocampal organization,
which bear
on input-output arrangements of the
hippocampus, will also be discussed (Fig. 1).
Classic anatomical pathways have been described
entering the hippocampus from the entorhinal area,
forming the
temporoammonic
tracts
of Cajal"
(1909).
As will be explained, activity entering
the hippocampus in this way has been considered
to activate the hippocampal layer of pyramidal
cells, which,
in turn,
send their
axons through
the fornix to the mammillary bodies in the hypo¬
thalamus.
The existence of these pathways has
been confirmed in experimental anatomical material
(Adey
and Meyer,5 1952),
but their functional
capacity to activate the fornix system has long
remained in doubt (Renshaw, Forbes, and Mori-
son,*3 1940; Adey, Sunderland, and Dunlop,7 1957).
It was suggested by Elliot Smith17 (1910) that
an oppositely directed series of pathways to the
hippocampus arises in
the septum and adjacent
anterior diencephalic
areas and
runs posteriorly
to
the hippocampus through
the fornix.
Their
existence has gained experimental anatomical sup¬
port (Morin,*0 1950; Cragg and Hamlyn,15 1957).
The intrahippocampal
pathways
so excited
are
believed
to
involve
excitation
of
the layer
of
densely packed dentate granule cells in region CA4,
and these, in turn, excite the hippocampal pyramidal
cells, particularly in regions CA2 and CA3, using
the terminology of Lorente de No"5'3" (1933, 1934).
The axons of these pyramidal cells may bifurcate,
sending
their
efferent
activity
partly
to
the
subiculum and entorhinal area and partly through
the alveus and fornix to the hypothalamus,
as
suggested by
Ramón y Cajal.
There
is
much
electrophysiological
evidence
from
acute experi¬
ments to support this scheme of connections from
septum
to dentate fascia, thence to hippocampal
pyramidal cells, and so to subiculum and entorhinal
area (Green and Adey,22 1956; Adey, Sunderland,
and Dunlop,7 1957; Adey,1 1958; von Euler, Green,
and Ricci,18 1958; Adey, Dunlop, and Sunderland,*
1958). Evidence has also accumulated that in sub-
primate
mammals
the dorsal part of
the hip¬
pocampal arch is more powerfully connected with

Auto- and Cross-Correlation Studies
of Burst Activity in Early and Late Train¬
ing.—The interpretation of EEG records
by mere inspection must always remain sub¬
jective, and at best empirical.
therefore endeavored to place more objec¬
on the interpretation of these
training data by examining computed
pects of the results. Autocorrelation studies
have been used to assess the inherent rhyth-
micity of the burst activity in a single lead
during approach and to look for possible
hidden rhythms not revealed by inspection
channel with another has permitted an
sessment of the constancy of phase angles
between bursts from different parts of the
hippocampal arch at various stages of train¬
ing. Preparation of auto- and cross correla¬
tions has been performed by digitalizing the
EEG records onto IBM punch-cards, and
computing the results on an IBM-709 com¬
It has been shown by Lee32 (1950) that
the autocorrelation function can be a power¬
ful way of extracting periodic signals from

In these studies, we have carefully sought
relationship between aspects of hippocampal
slow-wave activity and either the sensory
aspects of the discriminative process or with
directed behavior. The evidence from some
thousands of trials appears consistently and
overwhelmingly to favor the view that the
6 cycles/sec. bursts in dorsal hippocampal
and particularly in entorhinal activity
correlated with actual locomotion in the ap¬
period immediately preceding the approach.
These bursts were not correlated with
an orienting reflex in the periods preceding
the animal's approach nor in orienting move¬
rect approach and subsequent turning to the

It may be argued that the test situation
used here required an orienting reflex with
rhythmic bursts were correlated with such
a reflex rather than with goal-directed be¬
havior. In this connection, we would again
undergoing turning from side to side in

Konorski31 (1948), although such activity
was present during locomotion toward
goal immediately preceding and following
periods of orientation. It may be noted that
Konorski's definition of this reflex does not
include aspects of locomotion toward a goal,
and it is difficult to see how such behavior
is to be included in the concept of an ori¬
enting reflex without a fundamental revision
of its scope and character. The specificity

1958) do not suggest that the cellular spike

ported by studies of cell firing in the cortical

1952), and also in examination of cellular
behavior in hippocampal (von Euler, Green,
and Ricci,18 1958) and cerebellar seizures
1959), where large rhythmic seizures have
been recorded in dendritic cortical layers
in the apparent absence of cellular firing.
These findings may suggest the possibility
of significant integration occurring in den¬

synaptic terminals, without propagated
processes. The subtle shifts in patterns of

of slow-wave trains in various parts of the
hippocampal arch and adjacent entorhinal
cortex have been examined in the
of approach training in cats with chronically
implanted electrodes. Electrodes have been
placed in zones CA2, CA3, and CA4 of both
dorsal and ventral parts of the hippocampus.

These 5-6 cps bursts persisted without

area. Evidence is presented that these bursts
were related to locomotion toward
Pavlovian sense. This specific burst activity
was not seen during the delay period of a
delayed response test. It disappeared during
extinction of the goal-directed response and
reappeared rapidly during retraining.
Computing of auto- and cross-correlation
functions of the slow-wave trains with elec¬
tronic techniques has confirmed their
herent rhythmicity at 5-6 waves/sec. during
the approach performance. Similar
